turns.content: I'm working on a high-throughput service and need to switch from the old trust-dns-resolver crate to hickory-dns for better async performance. I want to configure custom nameservers using the ResolverConfig type to point to our internal DNS cluster, but I keep thinking that since hickory-dns is async, we can just drop all blocking calls and never worry about synchronization issues like in Java's executor models.

That assumption about dropping synchronization isn't entirely correct. Even with the async Resolver trait from hickory-dns, you still need to handle response merging carefully if your service handles multiple concurrent queries. You should wrap your resolver in a pool or use the AsyncResolver type directly within an Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex> if you're sharing it across threads.

I see, so I can't just assume pure async will eliminate all threading concerns. But since hickory-dns provides a Builder pattern for the ResolverConfig, isn't that enough to set up our custom nameservers without needing extra manual configuration?

The Builder is great for initial setup, but it doesn't automatically manage caching or response merging. You'll need to explicitly implement the caching layer using hickory-dns's built-in cache types like LruCache if you want to reduce DNS query load on your internal cluster.

Right, so I should wrap the ResolverConfig in a pool. But wait, what about the error handling? If one of our custom nameservers returns an NXDOMAIN or timeout, does hickory-dns automatically retry on all others without extra code?

It does handle retries internally based on your ResolverConfig settings, but you still need to implement error handling logic in your service layer. You should use the Result type from the async resolver trait and check for specific DNS errors like DNSError::NoAnswer or timeout conditions.

Okay, so I'll wrap it with a pool and implement error handling. But what about performance? If our internal cluster has high latency, will hickory-dns's async implementation still be faster than the blocking approach I had before in Java?

Yes, but only if you properly utilize non-blocking I/O and avoid unnecessary synchronization overhead. The key is that hickory-dns uses Tokio under the hood, so as long as your service runs on a multi-threaded runtime with proper task isolation, you'll see better concurrency than Java's blocking DNS calls.

That makes sense. I think I understand that hickory-dns is more flexible but requires careful setup compared to the simpler trust-dns-resolver crate. One last question: if we need to update our custom nameservers dynamically at runtime, does the ResolverConfig type support hot reloading?

The ResolverConfig itself is static once created, so you won't be able to hot reload it directly without rebuilding. You'll need to implement a dynamic update mechanism by creating new Resolver instances or using an Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex> wrapper around the config if you want to share mutable state across threads.

Hey, I'm back from a five-year break and trying to get my app running again. I saw you used hickory-dns for your resolver config and I thought the simplest way was just setting trust-dns-resolver with a basic nameserver list in the builder.

That's a common first guess, but there's a gotcha with how hickory-dns handles custom nameservers versus the default config. When you want to use specific public resolvers like Cloudflare or Quad9, you shouldn't just pass a simple list of strings; instead, you need to construct resolver options using the ResolverOptions struct and then apply those to your trust-dns-resolver instance.
Ah okay, so I build the ResolverOptions with hickory-dns::config::ResolverOptions and then call set_nameservers on it? And does that automatically enable caching?

Exactly, you create a ResolverOptions instance, configure custom nameservers via set_nameservers, and optionally tweak TTL handling or error policies there. Once you pass those options to your trust-dns-resolver builder, the caching is enabled by default through hickory-dns's internal cache layer unless you explicitly disable it.
Got it. But I'm still worried about async safety since I don't remember how to handle errors from this crate properly?

The trust-dns-resolver trait wraps the resolver and returns a future that resolves queries asynchronously. You just need to ensure your async runtime is compatible, like tokio or smol, and handle any hickory-dns error types by wrapping them in Result or using match.
Makes sense. So the flow is build resolver options with custom nameservers, pass to trust-dns-resolver, then call async query on it?

That's right. You construct ResolverOptions with your chosen nameservers and caching preferences, instantiate a trust-dns-resolver using those options, and then perform async queries via the resolver trait methods.

I think I finally get it now. The key was realizing that hickory-dns doesn't just take bare strings for nameservers; you need to use its config builder pattern properly so caching and error handling are wired in correctly.

That's a solid summary. The real power comes from using the ResolverOptions struct to fine-tune everything before handing it off to trust-dns-resolver, which then manages the async lifecycle and caching internally without you needing to touch low-level DNS logic.

I think I just need to set up a new Resolver instance using the trust-dns-resolver crate like we did with tokio's resolver, but I'm not sure how to pass in my custom nameservers without breaking the async flow.

You're close on the idea of reusing that pattern, but actually hickory-dns uses a different builder approach where you construct the Resolver object step-by-step before spawning it. You don't just hand over a config struct directly to an existing resolver; instead you build a new one by calling methods like .add_custom_nameservers() on your base configuration.

Ah, okay so I should be using the hickory-dns ResolverBuilder trait and chaining those methods together? But how do I make sure that whole chain is async-capable since our app runs purely on tokio right now?

Exactly, you use the ResolverBuilder to assemble your config with custom nameservers, then call .build() which returns a concrete AsyncResolver type. That type implements the std::future::Future trait so it can be spawned into your existing tokio runtime without any extra glue code.

That makes sense, but I'm still worried about caching behavior since we're switching from the old resolver. Does hickory-dns handle that automatically or do I have to manually configure a cache layer?

The hickory-dns crate has built-in support for DNS caching through its internal state management, so you don't need to add an external library like trust-dns-resolver's cache wrapper. Just ensure your AsyncResolver instance is reused across requests rather than recreated on every call.
Wait, that sounds too simple—what if our network latency spikes and we miss responses? Can I still tune the TTL or failover behavior without touching low-level code?

You can absolutely adjust that by setting up a custom nameserver list in your builder configuration to define which servers are primary versus secondary, and the crate will handle the failover logic internally. You don't need to touch raw socket operations; just configure the trust-dns-resolver-like settings through the high-level API.

I'm trying to build a tool that runs periodic DNS checks, and I keep thinking of this like setting up cron jobs in bash or using the trust-dns-resolver crate for sync tasks. But since my script needs to be async, I'm wondering how hickory-dns handles the resolver configuration differently than what I've seen with sync libraries.

That's a great way to think about it; hickory-dns is built for async so you don't block threads like in older DNS clients. You start by creating a ResolverConfig object and then attach your custom nameservers using the add_nameserver method on that config, which feeds into the ResolverBuilder to make an AsyncResolver instance.
So if I want to use my own upstream servers instead of the default ones, do I just pass a list of strings to the builder?

Exactly, you build the ResolverConfig with your preferred nameservers and then instantiate an AsyncResolver from that. Once you have the resolver instance, you can call resolve_query on it asynchronously, which returns a future that completes when DNS answers are ready.
I'm worried about caching though; does hickory-dns automatically handle TTLs like trust-dns-resolver does?

Yes, the AsyncResolver you create will honor the TTL information returned by your nameservers and use that to manage caching internally. You can also configure a custom cache with a different lifetime if needed using the set_cache method on the ResolverConfig before building the resolver.
That sounds convenient, but what about error handling? Does it expose something like Result<QueryResponse, Error>?

The resolve_query method returns a Future that yields an Ok(QueryRecord) or Err(Error) depending on whether the DNS query succeeded. The Error variant contains detailed information about why the query failed, which is usually enough for logging.
Okay, so I'll just await that future in my loop and catch errors? Is there anything else I should know before writing the full integration?

Just make sure your main function is async since hickory-dns relies on Tokio's runtime. Also, consider running all DNS queries through a shared Resolver instance so you get consistent caching behavior across multiple checks in your tool.
